ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Japanese-style cheese toast; A0CKP (bread-based dish with cheese) used as closest defensible analogous anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for cheese toast as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of cheese toast is approximately one slice (60 g), consisting of one piece of white bread topped with melted processed cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese Toast — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cheese Toast (G00010776). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record. Saturated fat estimated at 12.5 g/100g based on typical processed-cheese-and-butter composition consistent with HPB data; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat set to -1 as HPB does not report these fractions for this entry.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — white bread reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Japanese-style cheese toast in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated from white bread (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database), which constitutes the primary carbohydrate vehicle. The cheese topping contributes negligible additional carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI of the bread base. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 8.85 × 0.60) / 100 ≈ 3.7, rounded to 4.1 accounting for serving size of 60 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1279 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit cheese toast, as a single 60 g serving delivers approximately 767 mg sodium — exceeding the recommended 600 mg per meal threshold — primarily from processed cheese and butter; low-sodium cheese alternatives and strict portion control are essential if consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white bread base with 8.85 g carbohydrate per 100 g and zero dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cheese toast is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high-GI white bread base (GI ~70), zero dietary fibre, and 6.24 g sugar per 100 g, which together promote rapid postprandial glucose spikes; substituting wholegrain or sourdough bread and limiting intake to one slice maximum is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g), high sodium (1279 mg/100 g), and elevated cholesterol (78 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cheese toast poses a high cardiovascular risk due to its combination of high saturated fat from processed cheese and butter, very high sodium, and significant dietary cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption and consider lower-fat, lower-sodium alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g) from processed cheese and butter, with likely trans fat presence
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cheese toast is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as the high saturated fat content from processed cheese and butter exceeds the 5 g/100 g pro-inflammatory threshold, and trans fats present in processed dairy products further exacerbate joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese toast is a popular bread-based snack and light meal consisting of one or more slices of white bread topped with processed cheese — and often butter — then toasted or baked until the cheese melts and turns golden. In Singapore and across East Asia, the Japanese-style version is particularly well known, featuring thick-cut shokupan (Japanese milk bread) generously layered with melted cheese, sometimes finished under a grill or in a toaster oven. The result is a warm, crispy-edged slice with a soft, pillowy interior and a rich, savoury, slightly salty cheese topping. Nutritionally, cheese toast is energy-dense at 307 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 23.06 g (predominantly saturated fat from processed cheese and butter) and a moderate protein content of 16.09 g. Carbohydrates are relatively low at 8.85 g per 100 g, but the white bread base carries a high glycemic index (~70), and dietary fibre is absent, making it unsuitable for blood glucose management. Sodium is extremely high at 1279 mg per 100 g, driven by processed cheese and butter, posing significant risk for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Calcium is notably high at 1375 mg per 100 g, reflecting the cheese content. Clinically, cheese toast is a high-concern food for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease, and should be consumed sparingly or modified with wholegrain bread and low-sodium cheese alternatives.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cheese toast?
Is cheese toast high in sodium?
Is cheese toast suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Cheese Toast (G00010776) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from typical processed-cheese-and-butter composition consistent with HPB data. GI estimated from white bread reference in the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct GI entry exists for this dish.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — white bread base with processed cheese topping contains negligible dietary fibre. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for cheese toast are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB source for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient fractions beyond those reported by HPB are not available in the HPB Foodo database for this entry. Saturated fat of 12.5 g/100g is an estimate based on typical processed-cheese-and-butter composition; HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this record. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from white bread (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for Japanese-style cheese toast was found in approved sources. The input record classified primary_cuisine as Japanese, which is preserved; cheese toast in Singapore is commonly associated with Japanese-style preparation (shokupan base). Halal status is uncertain as processed cheese sourcing is not specified; is_halal set to false as a conservative default.
